perturbation.original_text: 5.4. Notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, each party's total liability in respect of damages under this Agreement, any regulation or common law shall be limited to the sum of all amounts received from Client in terms of this Agreement; provided, however, that this limitation shall not apply with respect to any claims arising out of or relating to clause 6 (Inventions and Proprietary Information), indemnification obligations or damages arising from a party's gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.4. Notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, each party's total liability in respect of damages under this Agreement, any regulation or common law shall be limited to the sum of all amounts received from Client in terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the exception for 'damages arising from a party's gross negligence or willful misconduct' allows a party to limit their liability even for egregious actions. This is against public policy and potentially violates laws that prohibit limiting liability for willful misconduct.
perturbation.law_explanation: Connecticut law does not allow parties to contractually limit liability for damages caused by reckless, willful, or wanton misconduct. Removing the exception means this agreement could attempt to limit liability even in cases of gross negligence, which contradicts Connecticut state law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: time of original wrong and trial court improperly applied doctrine in context of an alleged duty arising simply because of the existence of a relationship between the parents of minor children. 115 CA 404; judgment reversed, see 301 C. 575. The 3-year period begins on date the act or omission complained of occurs, not date the cause of action accrues or date the injury occurs. 117 CA 129. Legal malpractice claim which sounds in tort is governed by the 3-year statute of limitations. 134 CA 785; judgment affirmed, see 311 C. 282. Statute of limitations is tolled where attorney, in legal malpractice action, breached continuing duty to his clients to apprise them of his negligence in drafting deed and to cure problems resulting from such negligence while the opportunity existed. 142 CA 97. In time barred situation involving legal malpractice claims, burden is on plaintiff to establish that there is a genuine issue of material fact as to whether the statute of limitations was tolled by the continuous representation doctrine. 167 CA 691. Cited. 4 CS 222; Id., 293; 8 CS 302. Application of statute of limitations is that of the forum and not where the cause of action arose. 12 CS 477. Sec. 22-357, making the owner or keeper of a dog liable for any damage to person or property done by the dog, is controlled by a 3-year statute of limitations for an action founded upon a tort. 14 CS 428; 26 CS 294, 296. Action for inducing entry into a mental institution by fraud falls within 3-year statute of limitations. 15 CS 434. Where action charges breach of contract for failure to procure insurance coverage against damage to plaintiff's property, cause of action is in tort but is not one for injury to property under Sec. 52-584. 17 CS 62. Cited. 17 CS 114; Id., 407; 18 CS 225; Id., 310. Cause of action based on nuisance when not arising from negligence based on 3-year statute of limitations. 20 CS 35. Cited. 22 CS 300. Controlling statute of limitations for parents sued under Sec. 52-572. 24 CS 320. Impleading under Sec. 52-102a barred by statute of limitations; claim against manufacturer for injuries caused by defective skates held tort liability, not breach of implied warranty under Sec. 42a-2-725 of Uniform Commercial Code, and therefore barred by statute of limitations. 27 CS 46. Statute of limitations for tort actions applies to personal injury action based on breach of warranty. 28 CS 160. Action for tort limited to 3-year statute of limitations. 29 CS 71. Cited. Id., 72. Mental incompetence does not toll the time limitation, but imprisonment may. 31 CS 46. Cited. 34 CS 22; Id., 643, 646. 3-year statute of limitations for tort actions applies to fraud or deception action. 36 CS 277. Cited. Id., 357; 39 CS 458; 44 CS 207; Id., 477; Id., 527. Plaintiff failed to sufficiently allege fraudulent concealment to toll statute; defendant's assertion of statute of limitations in motion to strike rather than as a special defense was acceptable in this case; plaintiff failed to allege intent to conceal as is required to toll statute. 46 CS 386. Statutes of repose allow defendants at some point to be free from liability, absent unclean or fraudulent conduct, and logical conclusion is that legislature intended state to abide by statutes of repose. 51 CS 265. Cause of action brought under Sec. 52-572 is governed by 3-year limitation imposed by this section. 3 Conn. Cir. Ct. 379. (Return to Chapter Table of Contents) (Return to List of Chapters) (Return to List of Titles) Sec. 52-577a. Limitation of action based on product liability claim. (a) No product liability claim, as defined in section 52-572m , shall be brought but within three years from the date when the injury, death or property damage is first sustained or discovered or in the exercise of reasonable care should have been discovered, except that, subject to the provisions of subsections (c), (d) and (e) of this section, no such action may be brought against any party nor may any party be impleaded
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause excepting 'damages arising from a party's gross negligence or willful misconduct' directly contradicts the principle that parties cannot contractually limit liability for willful misconduct, as stated in the Law Explanation. While no specific Connecticut statute explicitly prohibits such limitations is provided in the scraped snippets, the general principle of not allowing limitation of liability for willful misconduct is a common law principle and removing this exception directly opposes it.

perturbation.original_text: 1.4. CRO agrees to perform the specific tasks set forth in the Project-specific Addenda in a professional manner, in strict accordance with the terms and conditions contained herein, relevant professional standards including in accordance with the Protocol and applicable amendments and ICH GCP Guidelines (ICH Harmonised Tripartite Guideline for Good Clinical Practice, May 1996) and all other relevant laws, rules, regulations and guidelines.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4. CRO agrees to perform the specific tasks set forth in the Project-specific Addenda in a professional manner, in accordance with the terms and conditions contained herein and the Protocol and applicable amendments.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'relevant professional standards including in accordance with ... ICH GCP Guidelines ... and all other relevant laws, rules, regulations and guidelines' weakens the commitment to legally required standards, particularly Good Clinical Practice (GCP) guidelines which are often mandated by regulatory bodies such as the FDA for clinical trials that may support approval in the US.  This introduces a legal risk if the trial does not adhere to the required guidelines.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR Part 312 outlines the FDA's requirements for Investigational New Drug Applications (IND). Clinical trials conducted under an IND must adhere to Good Clinical Practice (GCP) standards to ensure data integrity and subject safety. By removing the explicit reference to GCP guidelines and 'all other relevant laws, rules, regulations and guidelines,' the contract creates a potential legal contradiction, as it weakens the assurance that the clinical research will be conducted in accordance with FDA requirements for studies intended to be submitted to the FDA.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the explicit commitment to "relevant professional standards including in accordance with ... ICH GCP Guidelines ... and all other relevant laws, rules, regulations and guidelines". This omission weakens the assurance that clinical research will be conducted according to FDA requirements, particularly 21 CFR Part 312, which outlines requirements for Investigational New Drug Applications and mandates adherence to Good Clinical Practice (GCP) standards. The scraped snippet from eCFR :: 21 CFR Part 312 supports the importance of this regulation.
